evidence: src/thenvoi/adapters/codex/adapter.py contains many thin forwarding methods (_prepare_turn, _consume_turn_events, _handle_server_request, _handle_local_command, _emit_turn_outcome, etc.) that primarily delegate to helper modules without adding policy.

Codex helper modules define Protocols (CodexTurnStateProtocol, CodexCommandStateProtocol, CodexToolingStateProtocol) that depend on many private adapter attributes and Any-typed config, indicating indirection without a stable boundary contract.

src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py composes a large protocol lattice with dict[str, Any]-heavy signatures, while src/thenvoi/runtime/tools.py provides one concrete AgentTools implementation and tests/core/test_protocols.py validates structural conformance broadly rather than distinct implementations.

src/thenvoi/core/simple_adapter.py and src/thenvoi/core/control_plane_adapter.py preserve dual entrypoints via legacy arg-bag compatibility wrappers, and multiple adapters remain decorated with legacy_chat_turn_compat, extending long-term dual-surface complexity.

src/thenvoi/testing/runner_core.py and src/thenvoi/testing/example_runners.py pass dict[str, Any]/dict[str, object] and many injected callables through bootstrap paths, creating implicit parameter bags rather than typed startup contracts.

`tests/support/integration/config.py` is named as config but acts as a broad compatibility barrel: it re-exports fixtures, policy helpers, selection internals, cleanup errors, and cleanup orchestration in one module.

Holistic structure signals show `tests/support/integration/` as a high-traffic boundary (`avg_fan_in` 4.5; coupling edges from `tests/integration/` -> `tests/support/integration/` = 16), so ambiguous placement in this directory has subsystem-wide navigation impact.

`examples/common/scenario_registry.py` is a shared contract module imported across multiple example framework directories and test directories, but it lives under `examples/` (script-oriented namespace), which mixes executable example surface with reusable governance/configuration surface.

Adapter configuration shape is inconsistent across sibling adapters: `CodexAdapter` takes `config: CodexAdapterConfig`, while `AnthropicAdapter`, `PydanticAIAdapter`, `CrewAIAdapter`, and `ClaudeSDKAdapter` define `*AdapterConfig` dataclasses but expose large parameter-list constructors instead of consuming those config objects.

System-update formatting exists in multiple parallel abstractions (`SimpleAdapter.build_metadata_updates` and `thenvoi.adapters.system_updates.build_system_update_messages`) with overlapping responsibility and near-identical behavior.

Codex adapter behavior is decomposed across multiple helper modules with many pass-through methods in `adapter.py` (`_prepare_turn`, `_consume_turn_events`, `_handle_tool_call_request`, `_handle_local_command`, etc.), increasing navigation cost and blurring ownership boundaries.

findings.summary: Codex adapter keeps a high-cost wrapper chain where adapter methods mostly relay to helper modules through private-state Protocol seams.
evidence: CodexAdapter defines many methods whose body is only `return await <helper>(self, ...)` or `return <helper>(...)`, creating multiple call hops per behavior.
Helper Protocols require access to many adapter internals (`_client`, `_room_threads`, `_pending_approvals`, `_prompt_injected_rooms`, config Any), so the seam is not truly decoupled.
Behavioral responsibilities are split across files but still coupled by adapter private fields, increasing indirection without reducing dependency surface.
findings.suggestion: Replace Protocol-plus-forwarder pattern with a concrete `CodexSessionCoordinator` (or similar) owning typed state and operations; move turn/command/tool workflows behind that object and keep adapter as lifecycle shell only.

findings.summary: The tool protocol lattice is broad and Any-heavy despite effectively one operational implementation, reducing interface honesty.
evidence: Protocols compose multiple layers (Messaging/Participant/Contact/Memory/Schema/Dispatch/Agent) with many `dict[str, Any]` return and argument types.
AgentInput in core/types is typed to AgentToolsProtocol while runtime/tools provides one concrete AgentTools that mixes room/contact/memory/schema/dispatch responsibilities.
tests/core/test_protocols.py validates one dummy object against almost every protocol, signaling broad structural compatibility over focused boundary contracts.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to fewer capability interfaces that map to real extension points, introduce typed DTOs for high-traffic tool payloads, and keep internal-only operations concrete instead of protocolized.

findings.summary: Legacy arg-bag compatibility keeps a dual on_message API shape active across adapters, increasing abstraction overhead and ambiguity.
evidence: legacy_chat_turn_compat and legacy_control_turn_compat accept mixed inputs (`turn`, `msg`, `history`, `tools`, optional flags) and normalize at runtime.
Multiple adapters still use the decorator, so both typed context and legacy argument-bag invocation paths remain part of normal behavior.
normalize_* methods perform runtime type resolution/required-arg checks that would be unnecessary with a single canonical turn contract.
findings.suggestion: Define and publish a removal timeline for legacy arg-bag invocation, migrate adapters/tests to context-only invocation, and delete normalize/decorator compatibility paths after cutoff.

findings.summary: Runner bootstrap APIs rely on large callable injection surfaces and dict-based config bags, obscuring boundary contracts.
evidence: runner_core exposes bootstrap/build functions with many injectable callables and `dict[str, Any]` config flow across RepoInitializer/RunnerAgentFactory/RunnerLifecycleFn seams.
example_runners builds heterogeneous `dict[str, object]` kwargs from flags, coupling call sites to string-key contracts instead of typed runner params.
bootstrap.py uses `adapter: Any` and generic factory hooks, reinforcing weakly typed startup boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed runner request/plan dataclasses (per framework family) and narrow hook protocols to fewer explicit responsibilities; convert kwargs maps to typed constructor inputs.

findings.summary: Runtime root package still contains domain-specific compatibility shims that contradict the declared core-only boundary.
evidence: src/thenvoi/runtime/__init__.py docstring states domain helpers should come from dedicated submodules, but contact_tools.py and custom_tools.py remain in the runtime root namespace.

Both shim files explicitly redirect to deeper packages (thenvoi.runtime.contacts.contact_tools and thenvoi.runtime.tooling.custom_tools), showing root-level placement is now compatibility residue rather than ownership.
tests/runtime/test_custom_tools.py already uses canonical subpackage import (thenvoi.runtime.tooling.custom_tools), indicating callers have moved while root shims persist.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: add a dedicated legacy boundary package (for example `src/thenvoi/runtime/compat/legacy_modules/`) and move compatibility shim ownership there while leaving temporary import stubs at current paths. Stage 2: update shim registration maps and any remaining imports to canonical modules (`thenvoi.runtime.contacts.*`, `thenvoi.runtime.tooling.*`). Stage 3: remove root stubs after deprecation window. Validate with `uv run ruff check .`, `uv run pyrefly check`, and `uv run pytest tests/runtime/test_custom_tools.py -v`.

findings.summary: Repository-root test config owns integration-specific plugin wiring, blurring test package boundaries.
evidence: tests/conftest.py globally loads tests.support.integration.plugin and tests.support.integration.fixtures at root scope.
tests/runtime/test_execution.py and tests/test_agent.py are non-integration suites under different concerns, but root plugin loading couples them to integration package initialization.
The root conftest function also applies integration collection filtering, reinforcing that integration concerns currently sit in the top-level test boundary instead of an integration-scoped boundary.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: split `tests/conftest.py` into root-only shared hooks and `tests/integration/conftest.py` for integration plugins/guards. Stage 2: keep only truly global fixtures in root and move integration plugin registration to integration scope. Stage 3: run `uv run pytest tests/test_agent.py tests/runtime/test_execution.py -v` and `uv run pytest tests/integration/ -v --collect-only` to verify boundary behavior, then run `uv run ruff check .` to catch stale imports.

findings.summary: Integration governance contracts are concentrated in a misnamed compatibility barrel (`config.py`).
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` re-exports unrelated concerns (fixtures, policy enforcement, selection internals, cleanup exceptions/functions) and adds wrapper indirection; this is broader than a config module boundary.

`pyproject.toml` defines integration marker policy at repo level (`integration`, `requires_api`, `requires_multi_agent`, `requires_user_api`), while marker composition lives separately in `tests/support/integration/markers.py`; consumers still route through `config.py` for many non-config contracts.

History-converter integration helpers (`tests/integration/history_converters/support.py`) also define integration lifecycle helpers (`no_clean`, chat setup/cleanup), indicating contract ownership is not centralized by concern.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `tests/support/integration/contracts/` with focused modules `markers.py`, `cleanup.py`, `settings.py`, `selection.py`; (2) move integration-lifecycle helpers from `tests/integration/history_converters/support.py` into `tests/support/integration/contracts/history.py` and keep payload builders in a test-local helper if domain-specific; (3) replace `tests/support/integration/config.py` with a thin deprecated facade that only re-exports from `contracts/*` and add a removal timeline docstring; (4) update imports with `rg -l "tests\.support\.integration\.config" tests | xargs sed -i '' 's/tests\.support\.integration\.config/tests.support.integration.contracts.<module>/g'` (module-specific); (5) validate with `uv run pytest tests/test_integration_policy_contract.py tests/test_integration_cleanup_policy.py tests/integration/ -m integration -v` and `uv run thenvoi-quality-gates`.

findings.summary: Reusable scenario contract data is placed under `examples/`, conflating script and shared package roles.
evidence: `examples/common/scenario_registry.py` defines canonical cross-framework keys/messages/adapter kwargs used by multiple framework example directories and by tests, but its location under `examples/` implies script/demo ownership rather than shared contract ownership.
`README.md` presents framework coverage and quality-gate governance as repository-level contracts; the scenario registry participates in that contract but is not in a governance/test-support package.

Holistic structure shows `examples/common/` imported by multiple sibling example directories and tests (broad reuse), which is a placement signal for a dedicated shared-support package rather than a script tree.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) add `src/thenvoi/example_support/scenarios.py` (or `tests/support/examples/scenario_registry.py` if strictly test/example scope) as the canonical shared registry location; (2) move registry constants/functions from `examples/common/scenario_registry.py` to the new target; (3) keep `examples/common/scenario_registry.py` as temporary shim importing from the new path; (4) update imports in example and test modules (`rg -l "examples\.common\.scenario_registry" examples tests`) to the new module; (5) validate with `uv run pytest tests/example_agents/ -v tests/docs/test_readme_package_structure.py` and `uv run thenvoi-quality-gates`.

findings.summary: Sibling adapters expose conflicting configuration patterns despite shared config dataclasses.
evidence: `CodexAdapter.__init__` accepts `config: CodexAdapterConfig | None` and uses `self.config` as the canonical settings object.

`AnthropicAdapterConfig`, `PydanticAIAdapterConfig`, `CrewAIAdapterConfig`, and `ClaudeSDKAdapterConfig` are defined/exported, but their adapters still use long keyword-heavy constructors and assign fields directly (`self.model`, `self.custom_section`, etc.) rather than accepting config objects.
Public exports in `adapters/__init__.py` suggest a unified config model, but runtime construction semantics differ by adapter family.
findings.suggestion: Standardize adapter construction around one contract: either (A) all adapters accept a `config` object as primary API with backward-compatible kwargs shim, or (B) remove/privatize unused config dataclasses. Prefer option A and migrate adapters incrementally with deprecation warnings for direct kwargs.

findings.summary: System metadata injection is duplicated across base and helper layers.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter` already implements `build_metadata_updates`/`apply_metadata_updates` with `[System]: ...` formatting.
`adapters/system_updates.py` re-implements equivalent update message construction and append/prepend helpers.
Adapters/processors mix usage patterns (e.g., Claude SDK uses `build_metadata_updates`, CrewAI processing uses `apply_metadata_updates`), leaving two canonical sources for the same policy.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single canonical system-update API owned by `SimpleAdapter` (or a dedicated shared utility, not both). Make all adapters call that API and remove duplicate helpers to avoid drift in prefixing/order semantics.

findings.summary: Codex adapter splits one workflow across too many thin modules and pass-through methods.
evidence: `adapter.py` defines numerous thin wrappers that only delegate to helper functions (`prepare_turn`, `consume_turn_events`, approval handlers, command handlers, tooling handlers).

Helper modules depend on broad Protocols with private adapter internals (`_client`, `_pending_approvals`, `_prompt_injected_rooms`, etc.), so responsibilities are split physically but still tightly coupled logically.
Understanding a single turn path requires jumping across multiple files plus Protocol contracts, increasing indirection cost without clear boundary isolation.
findings.suggestion: Refactor Codex flow into explicit cohesive components (e.g., `TurnOrchestrator`, `ApprovalManager`, `ToolExecutor`) with narrow public state and concrete constructor dependencies, then remove pass-through wrappers in `adapter.py`.

findings.summary: Runtime error-recording pattern is duplicated instead of reused, creating inconsistent semantics.
evidence: `NonFatalErrorRecorder` provides shared nonfatal storage and logging behavior and is used in `ExecutionContext`.
`RoomPresence` reimplements separate `_nonfatal_errors` storage and `_record_nonfatal_error` logic instead of reusing the shared mixin.
This creates divergent logging/detail behavior between runtime components that should follow one recoverable-error contract.
findings.suggestion: Adopt `NonFatalErrorRecorder` in `RoomPresence` and remove local duplicate machinery; centralize context formatting policy in the mixin so runtime components emit consistent nonfatal diagnostics.
